title: W7 A Differentiation Multidim Spaces
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW‚ÄďSydney

Week 7A ‚ÄĒ Term 1, 2026

1/9  Differentiation in Rn

2/9   Derivatives as Linear Approximations

Definition 1

Let I be an open interval and f : I ‚Üí R. Say that f is differentiable at x ‚ąą I if there is a linear mapping T : R ‚Üí R such that f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x) lim = 0. u‚Üíx u‚ąíx

In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x and denoted by T = Df (x).

We want to extend this concept to functions defined on open sets in Rn :

f (x) = f (x1 , x2 , , xn ).

3/9   Derivatives in Rn as Linear Approximations

Definition 2

Let S be an open subset of Rn and f : S ‚Üí R. Say f is differentiable at x ‚ąą S if there is a linear mapping T : Rn ‚Üí R such that

|f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| lim = 0. u‚Üíx ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•

In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x and denoted by T = Df (x).

Remark ‚ó¶ Note u ‚Üí x but there is not a single direction of approximation to x.

4/9   The Derivative of a Linear Function

Example 1

Let f : Rn ‚Üí R be a linear function. We can represent f via a (column) vector a ‚ąą Rn such that

f (x) = a ¬∑ x = a‚ä§ x, for all x ‚ąą Rn .

5/9   The Derivative of a Linear Function

Example 1

Let f : Rn ‚Üí R be a linear function. We can represent f via a (column) vector a ‚ąą Rn such that

f (x) = a ¬∑ x = a‚ä§ x, for all x ‚ąą Rn .

Claim Df (x) = a‚ä§ , for all x ‚ąą Rn .

5/9   The Derivative of a Linear Function

Example 1

Let f : Rn ‚Üí R be a linear function. We can represent f via a (column) vector a ‚ąą Rn such that

f (x) = a ¬∑ x = a‚ä§ x, for all x ‚ąą Rn .

Claim Df (x) = a‚ä§ , for all x ‚ąą Rn .

Show Verify that Df (x) satisfies the definition: |f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| |a‚ä§ u ‚ąí a‚ä§ x ‚ąí a‚ä§ (u ‚ąí x)| lim = lim u‚Üíx ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• u‚Üíx ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• = 0.

Claim Dg(x) = 2x‚ä§ , for all x ‚ąą Rn .

6/9

Claim Dg(x) = 2x‚ä§ , for all x ‚ąą Rn .

Show First compute g(u) ‚ąí g(x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x) = u‚ä§ u ‚ąí x‚ä§ x ‚ąí 2x‚ä§ (u ‚ąí x) = u‚ä§ u ‚ąí 2u‚ä§ x + x‚ä§ x = (u ‚ąí x)‚ä§ (u ‚ąí x) = ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•2 .

6/9

Claim Dg(x) = 2x‚ä§ , for all x ‚ąą Rn .

Show First compute g(u) ‚ąí g(x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x) = u‚ä§ u ‚ąí x‚ä§ x ‚ąí 2x‚ä§ (u ‚ąí x) = u‚ä§ u ‚ąí 2u‚ä§ x + x‚ä§ x = (u ‚ąí x)‚ä§ (u ‚ąí x) = ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•2 .

Now verify that Dg(x) satisfies the definition. |g(u) ‚ąí g(x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•2 lim = lim u‚Üíx ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• u‚Üíx ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•

= lim ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• = 0. u‚Üíx

6/9   The Derivative is Unique

Suppose f is differentiable at x ‚ąą S ‚äÜ Rn . ‚ó¶ Denote the derivative of f at x by Df (x).

‚ó¶ Df (x) is represented by a 1 √ó n matrix (a row vector).

‚ó¶ The gradient vector ‚ąáf (x) is defined as the transpose of Df (x).

‚ó¶ Since ‚ąáf (x) = Df (x)‚ä§ , it is a column vector, thus an element of Rn .

7/9   The Derivative is Unique

Suppose f is differentiable at x ‚ąą S ‚äÜ Rn . ‚ó¶ Denote the derivative of f at x by Df (x).

‚ó¶ Df (x) is represented by a 1 √ó n matrix (a row vector).

‚ó¶ The gradient vector ‚ąáf (x) is defined as the transpose of Df (x).

‚ó¶ Since ‚ąáf (x) = Df (x)‚ä§ , it is a column vector, thus an element of Rn .

Proposition 1

Let S ‚äÜ Rn be open. If f : S ‚Üí R is differentiable at x ‚ąą S, then its derivative is unique.

Proof Omitted.

7/9   Differentiation and Continuity

Proposition 2

If f : S ‚Üí R is differentiable at x ‚ąą S, there are positive real numbers őī1 and K such that ‚ą•u‚ąíx‚ą• < őī1 implies |f (u) ‚ąí f (x)| ‚Č§ K ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•.

Intuitively, if f is differentiable at x, then it has a bounded slope at x.

8/9   Differentiation and Continuity

Proposition 2

If f : S ‚Üí R is differentiable at x ‚ąą S, there are positive real numbers őī1 and K such that ‚ą•u‚ąíx‚ą• < őī1 implies |f (u) ‚ąí f (x)| ‚Č§ K ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą•.

Intuitively, if f is differentiable at x, then it has a bounded slope at x.

Proposition 3

If f : S ‚Üí R is differentiable at x ‚ąą S, the f is continuous at x.

Proof Fix ŌĶ > 0 and let 0 < őī ‚Č§ min{ KŌĶ , őī1 }, where őī1 and K are as in the above proposition. For all u ‚ąą S with ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• < őī, we have

|f (u) ‚ąí f (x)| ‚Č§ K ‚ą•u ‚ąí x‚ą• < Kőī ‚Č§ ŌĶ. ‚Ė†

8/9   Examples

Example 3

Let S = Rn ++ and f : S ‚Üí R be defined by

f (x) = xőĪ 1 őĪ2 őĪn 1 x2 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ xn .

The derivative of f at x ‚ąą S is the linear map Df (x) represented by the gradient vector   őĪ1 ‚ąáf (x) = x1 f (x), őĪ x2 f (x), , őĪ xn n f (x) .

Of course this function is widely employed in economics. We will later use a different method to verify that its derivative has the form given above.
